seoTitle: How to Create User Stories in Trello
metaDescription: Write agile user stories on Trello cards using the standard template format, Custom Fields for story points, and acceptance criteria checklists.
intro: Translating user needs into development-ready tasks works best with a consistent structure your whole team recognizes. This guide covers formatting user stories on Trello cards, tracking story points, and building acceptance criteria checklists.

steps.heading: Set up a backlog list
steps.body: Create a dedicated list to hold your product backlog before writing individual stories.

steps.heading: Format your story title
steps.body: Use the industry-standard structure: 'As a [user], I want [action] so that [benefit]'.

steps.heading: Add Story Points as a Custom Field
steps.body: Create a Custom Field to track story point estimates for each card.

steps.heading: Track acceptance criteria
steps.body: Use another Custom Field or a checklist to capture the specific acceptance criteria for the story.

steps.heading: Build a reusable template
steps.body: Design a description template inside your backlog list so you can quickly spawn new, standardized stories.

steps.heading: Label by epic or component
steps.body: Apply high-contrast labels to segment stories by feature epics, bugs, or platform components.

faq.question: What's the standard format for a user story?
faq.answer: The common template is: 'As a [user], I want [action] so that [benefit]', which keeps the focus on user value.

faq.question: How do I track story points in Trello?
faq.answer: You can create a Custom Field specifically for Story Points to keep estimates visible on each card.

faq.question: Can I reuse the same story format for every card?
faq.answer: Yes, building a reusable description template in your backlog list lets you spawn new, standardized stories quickly.

faq.question: How do I organize stories by feature?
faq.answer: Use high-contrast labels to group stories by epic, bug type, or platform component.
